_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All certified organic farms must submit annual soil reports. Greenfield Farm has not submitted a soil report this year.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
items.ChoiceA: Greenfield Farm does not grow any organic produce.
items.ChoiceB: Greenfield Farm will lose its certification next year.
items.ChoiceC: Most certified organic farms submit their reports on time.
items.ChoiceD: Greenfield Farm is not currently a certified organic farm.
items.ChoiceE: The soil report requirement is new this year.
items.WhyWrongA: Growing organic produce does not require certification — a farm can grow organic produce without being certified. Out of scope.
items.WhyWrongB: Future consequences (losing certification) go beyond what the stimulus supports — too extreme / not entailed.
items.WhyWrongC: 'Most farms' is a quantifier claim not supported by the stimulus, which says only 'all certified farms must submit.'
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Via contrapositive: If a farm does not submit a soil report → it is not a certified organic farm. Greenfield has not submitted → Greenfield is not certified. Valid deductive inference.
items.WhyWrongE: The newness of the requirement is not stated or inferable from the premises.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Some of the paintings in the museum were donated by private collectors. All paintings donated by private collectors have been authenticated. No painting that has been authenticated is available for loan.
items.Stem: Which of the following can be properly concluded from the statements above?
items.ChoiceA: No private collector has donated a painting to any other museum.
items.ChoiceB: Some authenticated paintings were donated by private collectors.
items.ChoiceC: None of the paintings in the museum are available for loan.
items.ChoiceD: Some paintings in the museum are not available for loan.
items.ChoiceE: All paintings in the museum have been authenticated.
items.WhyWrongA: Other museums are entirely out of scope.
items.WhyWrongB: This reverses the direction: we know collector donations → authenticated, not that authenticated → collector donation. Reversal trap.
items.WhyWrongC: We know only that some paintings are not available for loan (those donated by collectors); we cannot conclude none are — too extreme.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Some paintings → donated by collectors → authenticated → not available for loan. Via chained inference, some paintings are not available for loan. 'Some' claims survive the chain.
items.WhyWrongE: Only some paintings were donated; we cannot conclude all are authenticated — too extreme.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Every employee who completed the safety course received a certificate. No one who received a certificate was assigned to the night shift.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
items.ChoiceA: Everyone assigned to the night shift received a certificate.
items.ChoiceB: Some employees who received certificates work the night shift.
items.ChoiceC: All night-shift employees completed the safety course.
items.ChoiceD: No employee who completed the safety course was assigned to the night shift.
items.WhyWrongB: This contradicts the premise that certificate-holders are not on the night shift.
items.WhyWrongC: This reverses the reasoning; nothing links night-shift workers to the course.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Completed course -> certificate -> not night shift. Chaining the conditionals, no course-completer is on the night shift.
items.WhyWrongE: Only course-completers are said to receive certificates; not all employees.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All items on the top shelf are fragile. Nothing fragile may be handled by visitors. The blue vase is on the top shelf.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must also be true?
items.ChoiceA: Some items not on the top shelf are fragile.
items.ChoiceB: Visitors may handle any item that is not fragile.
items.ChoiceC: The blue vase may not be handled by visitors.
items.ChoiceD: Only fragile items are placed on the top shelf.
items.ChoiceE: Everything that is fragile is on the top shelf.
items.WhyWrongA: Nothing is said about fragile items elsewhere; this is not entailed.
items.WhyWrongB: Other rules could forbid handling non-fragile items; this is not entailed.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Top shelf → fragile → not handled by visitors. The vase is on the top shelf, so it may not be handled. Valid chain.
items.WhyWrongD: The premises don't say the top shelf holds only fragile items.
items.WhyWrongE: This reverses 'top shelf → fragile'; fragile things could be elsewhere too.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All members of the choir can read music. Some members of the choir are under eighteen. Everyone who can read music has taken a theory class.
items.Stem: Which one of the following must be true on the basis of the statements above?
items.ChoiceA: Some people under eighteen have taken a theory class.
items.ChoiceB: All people under eighteen can read music.
items.ChoiceC: Everyone who has taken a theory class can read music.
items.ChoiceD: Most members of the choir are under eighteen.
items.ChoiceE: No one over eighteen belongs to the choir.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Some under-eighteen choir members → can read music → have taken a theory class. 'Some' survives the chain.
items.WhyWrongB: The claim runs from choir members, not from all under-eighteens; unsupported.
items.WhyWrongE: Nothing rules out over-eighteen choir members.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The store sells only shoes and hats. Every hat it sells costs less than $30. The store sells one item that costs $45.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?
items.ChoiceA: The store's single most expensive item is a pair of shoes.
items.ChoiceB: The store sells at least one pair of shoes that costs $45.
items.ChoiceC: Every pair of shoes the store sells costs more than $30.
items.ChoiceD: Every pair of shoes costs more than every hat.
items.ChoiceE: The store sells no item costing less than $30.
items.WhyWrongA: Another item could tie or exceed $45; being most expensive is not entailed.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The $45 item can't be a hat (hats are under $30) and the store sells only shoes and hats, so it must be a pair of shoes.
items.WhyWrongC: Other shoes could cost any amount; this is not entailed.
items.WhyWrongD: No comparison between all shoes and all hats is supported.
items.WhyWrongE: This contradicts the premise that hats cost less than $30.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] In the tournament, every player who won their first match advanced to the second round. Dana did not advance to the second round. Every player played a first match.
items.Stem: Which one of the following must be true?
items.ChoiceA: Dana did not play a first match.
items.ChoiceB: Everyone who advanced to the second round won their first match.
items.ChoiceC: Every player who lost a first match failed to advance.
items.ChoiceD: Dana was the only player who failed to advance.
items.ChoiceE: Dana did not win her first match.
items.WhyWrongA: This contradicts the premise that every player played a first match.
items.WhyWrongB: This reverses the rule; players might advance by other means too.
items.WhyWrongC: The rule covers winners, not losers; non-winners advancing is not excluded.
items.WhyWrongD: Nothing says Dana was the only non-advancer.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Won first match → advanced. Dana did not advance, so by contrapositive she did not win her first match.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Every grant application that was approved included a budget. Some applications that included a budget were submitted late. No late application was approved.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must also be true?
items.ChoiceA: Some approved applications were submitted late.
items.ChoiceB: All applications that included a budget were approved.
items.ChoiceD: Every application submitted on time was approved.
items.ChoiceE: Some applications that included a budget were not approved.
items.WhyWrongA: This contradicts the premise that no late application was approved.
items.WhyWrongB: The premises give no basis for approving all budget applications.
items.WhyWrongC: The proportion including a budget is not established.
items.WhyWrongD: On-time status does not guarantee approval; unsupported.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Some budget applications were late, and no late application was approved; those late budget applications were therefore not approved.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Whenever the alarm is set, the door locks automatically. Last night the door did not lock automatically.
items.Stem: Which one of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?
items.ChoiceA: The door can be locked only by setting the alarm.
items.ChoiceB: The alarm was not set last night.
items.ChoiceC: Setting the alarm is the only thing that locks the door.
items.ChoiceD: The alarm is never set at night.
items.ChoiceE: The door remained unlocked throughout the night.
items.WhyWrongA: This reverses the conditional; other means of locking aren't excluded.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Alarm set → door locks. The door did not lock, so by contrapositive the alarm was not set.
items.WhyWrongC: Same reversal — the premise doesn't make the alarm the only cause.
items.WhyWrongD: A single night tells us nothing about the alarm's general nighttime use.
items.WhyWrongE: The door might have been locked manually; 'did not lock automatically' ≠ 'unlocked all night.'

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Most of the apartments in the building have balconies. All apartments that have balconies face the park.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?
items.ChoiceA: The apartments that lack balconies face away from the park.
items.ChoiceB: Most of the apartments in the building face the park.
items.ChoiceC: Some apartments without balconies face the park.
items.ChoiceD: All apartments that face the park have balconies.
items.ChoiceE: All of the apartments in the building face the park.
items.WhyWrongA: Also unsupported — their orientation is simply unknown.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. More than half have balconies, and all balcony apartments face the park, so more than half — i.e., most — face the park.
items.WhyWrongC: Nothing is established about balcony-less apartments.
items.WhyWrongD: This reverses the conditional; park-facing apartments need not have balconies.
items.WhyWrongE: The balcony-less apartments' orientation is unknown, so 'all' is not entailed.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] No reptile is warm-blooded. All mammals are warm-blooded. The Gila monster is a reptile.
items.Stem: Which one of the following must be true?
items.ChoiceA: Every animal is either a reptile or a mammal.
items.ChoiceE: The Gila monster is not a mammal.
items.WhyWrongA: Nothing rules out animals that are neither reptiles nor mammals.
items.WhyWrongB: This contradicts the premises: reptiles aren't warm-blooded, mammals are.
items.WhyWrongC: This reverses the relationship; being cold-blooded does not make something a reptile.
items.WhyWrongD: This contradicts 'no reptile is warm-blooded' applied to a reptile.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. The Gila monster is a reptile, hence not warm-blooded; but all mammals are warm-blooded, so it is not a mammal.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Every dish on the lunch menu contains cheese, mushrooms, or both. The ravioli contains no mushrooms.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?
items.ChoiceA: No dish on the menu contains both cheese and mushrooms.
items.ChoiceC: The ravioli is the only dish on the menu that contains cheese.
items.ChoiceD: The ravioli contains only cheese and nothing else.
items.ChoiceE: Every dish containing cheese lacks mushrooms.
items.WhyWrongA: This contradicts the premise, which explicitly allows both.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Each dish has cheese or mushrooms (or both); the ravioli has no mushrooms, so it must contain cheese.
items.WhyWrongC: Other dishes could contain cheese as well.
items.WhyWrongD: It could contain other ingredients too; 'only cheese' is not entailed.
items.WhyWrongE: This over-generalizes to all cheese dishes.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All code that passed review was deployed. Some code that was deployed contained a bug. Any code that contains a bug must be revised.
items.Stem: Which one of the following must be true?
items.ChoiceA: All deployed code must be revised.
items.ChoiceB: Code that was not deployed contains no bugs.
items.ChoiceC: Some code that was deployed must be revised.
items.ChoiceD: All code that passed review contained a bug.
items.ChoiceE: Any code that must be revised contains a bug.
items.WhyWrongA: Only some deployed code is said to contain a bug, so 'all' overreaches.
items.WhyWrongB: Undeployed code's bug status is not addressed.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Some deployed code contains a bug, and buggy code must be revised, so some deployed code must be revised.
items.WhyWrongD: Nothing links passing review to containing a bug.
items.WhyWrongE: This reverses the rule; things could require revision for other reasons.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Every violinist in the orchestra can read sheet music. Most people who can read sheet music also play piano. No one in the orchestra plays more than two instruments.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must also be true?
items.ChoiceA: Most violinists in the orchestra play piano.
items.ChoiceB: Some people who play piano are violinists in the orchestra.
items.ChoiceC: No violinist in the orchestra plays piano.
items.ChoiceD: Every violinist in the orchestra plays piano.
items.ChoiceE: At least one violinist in the orchestra can read sheet music.
items.WhyWrongA: 'Most sheet-music readers' need not translate into 'most violinists' — the violinists could be among the minority.
items.WhyWrongB: Nothing establishes that any orchestra violinist is among the piano-playing readers.
items.WhyWrongC: This is unsupported; some violinists may well play piano.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Most' sheet-music readers play piano; that does not force every violinist to.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Every violinist reads sheet music and the orchestra has violinists, so at least one reader exists.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Some of the grants awarded this year went to first-time applicants. All grants awarded this year required a detailed budget. No first-time applicant had submitted a budget before.
items.Stem: Which one of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?
items.ChoiceA: No experienced applicant submitted a detailed budget.
items.ChoiceB: Most grants this year went to first-time applicants.
items.ChoiceC: Every first-time applicant received a grant this year.
items.ChoiceD: All detailed budgets came from first-time applicants.
items.ChoiceE: Some who submitted a detailed budget had never submitted one before.
items.WhyWrongA: Experienced applicants' budgets are not restricted by the premises.
items.WhyWrongC: 'Some grants went to first-timers' does not mean every first-timer got one.
items.WhyWrongD: Grants also went to non-first-timers, who submitted budgets too.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Some grantees were first-timers; all grantees submitted a budget; first-timers had never submitted one before — so some budget-submitters were first-time submitters.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All of the museum's Roman coins are on the second floor. Most of the coins on the second floor are gold. None of the museum's gold coins are replicas.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?
items.ChoiceA: All of the museum's gold coins are Roman.
items.ChoiceB: Every coin on the second floor is a replica.
items.ChoiceC: Most of the museum's Roman coins are gold.
items.ChoiceD: None of the Roman coins are gold.
items.ChoiceE: Some coins on the second floor are not replicas.
items.WhyWrongA: Gold coins need not be Roman; they are simply on the second floor.
items.WhyWrongB: This contradicts that gold coins (which exist there) are not replicas.
items.WhyWrongC: The Roman coins could be among the second-floor minority that is not gold.
items.WhyWrongD: Nothing forces the Roman coins to be non-gold.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Most second-floor coins are gold, and no gold coin is a replica, so some second-floor coins are not replicas.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] No reptiles are warm-blooded. Some animals in the exhibit are reptiles. All warm-blooded animals in the exhibit are fed twice daily.
items.Stem: Which one of the following must be true on the basis of the statements above?
items.ChoiceA: No animal in the exhibit is fed twice daily.
items.ChoiceB: Every reptile in the exhibit is fed twice daily.
items.ChoiceC: Some animals in the exhibit are not fed twice daily on account of being warm-blooded.
items.ChoiceD: Some animals in the exhibit are not warm-blooded.
items.ChoiceE: All animals in the exhibit are reptiles.
items.WhyWrongA: Warm-blooded animals there are fed twice daily, so this is false.
items.WhyWrongB: Feeding twice daily is stated only for warm-blooded animals, and reptiles are not warm-blooded.
items.WhyWrongC: The feeding schedule is not tied to the reptiles' status this way; this misstates the link.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Some exhibit animals are reptiles, and no reptile is warm-blooded, so some exhibit animals are not warm-blooded.
items.WhyWrongE: Only 'some' animals are reptiles, not all.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Every artifact in the sealed chamber predates the flood. Some artifacts that predate the flood bear the royal seal. No artifact bearing the royal seal was made outside the capital.
items.Stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must also be true?
items.ChoiceA: Some of the artifacts in the sealed chamber bear the royal seal.
items.ChoiceB: Every artifact that was made in the capital bears the royal seal.
items.ChoiceC: No artifact in the sealed chamber was made outside the capital.
items.ChoiceD: Any artifact in the sealed chamber that bears the royal seal was made in the capital.
items.ChoiceE: Every artifact that predates the flood was made in the capital.
items.WhyWrongA: The sealed artifacts that predate the flood need not be the ones inside the chamber.
items.WhyWrongB: This reverses the rule; something could be made in the capital without a seal.
items.WhyWrongC: Only sealed artifacts must come from the capital; an unsealed chamber artifact could be from anywhere.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Such an artifact bears the royal seal, and no royal-seal artifact was made outside the capital, so it was made there.
items.WhyWrongE: Only the sealed flood-predating artifacts must be from the capital, not all of them.
